title: Drone-UGV Disaster Response System
desc: A disaster response simulation where drones and ground robots work together to find and rescue people, using MAPPO and QMIX with a built-in fairness reward.

detail.body:
April 2026 · Robotics · Reinforcement Learning · Personal Research

View the full project, code, and documentation: https://github.com/jackpham-rgb/drone-ugv-disaster-response

My research portal: https://jackpham-rgb.github.io/drone-ugv-disaster-response/index.html

[SECTION: What this is]

This project is a disaster response simulation where drones and ground robots work together to find and rescue people.

Instead of having every robot do everything, I split the system into roles:
- Drones scout and map the environment
- Ground robots handle rescue
- Fire trucks control fire spread

[SECTION: Why I built this]

This is a follow-up to my earlier research. Before, all robots had the same role. I wanted to try a different idea where each type of agent has a clear responsibility. It makes the system feel more realistic and also easier to scale.

I also wanted to explore how reinforcement learning and fairness can affect coordination between agents.

[SECTION: What I learned]

I learned that coordination becomes more interesting when agents do not have full information.

In this system, drones see everything while ground robots only see what drones discover. This made the problem less about control and more about information flow and decision making.

I also learned how fairness can be built directly into learning, not just as a separate rule.

[SECTION: What is different from the original approach]

The original REU (2024) used single-tier robots with FE-MADDPG. This project adds:
- Architecture: Two-tier UAV + UGV + FireTruck
- Drone policy: MAPPO (CTDE framework)
- Ground policy: QMIX with embedded fairness term
- Fairness reward: Built into QMIX Q-value directly
- Environment: 3D isometric, dynamic fire spread
- Fire model: Cellular automaton with wind
- Terrain: Procedural mountain or urban biome

The fairness reward is still present from the original paper, just wired differently:

r = ε + |eᵢ / ē − 1| / ē

In the original, this drove the FE-MADDPG policy gradient. Here it is subtracted directly from the QMIX per-agent Q-value, so rescue robots that already have more completed rescues get penalized when bidding for new tasks.

[SECTION: Full system simulation]

Everything runs together in a 3D simulation where you can switch policies, change the number of agents, adjust fire conditions, and run and compare performance.

[IMAGE: assets/img/ugv-simulation-view.png | Full system running in the 3D simulation]

You can run the simulation directly in the browser: https://jackpham-rgb.github.io/drone-ugv-disaster-response/sim3d_v3.html

Research notebook: https://github.com/jackpham-rgb/drone-ugv-disaster-response/blob/main/drone_ugv_notebook.ipynb

[SECTION: Conclusion]

This project helped me see how multi-agent systems behave when information is limited and roles are separated.

These ideas can be used in robotics, distributed systems, and large-scale coordination problems where agents need to work together with partial information.
detail.status: Completed. This is a personal extension of my earlier research work.
